# Matplotlib - chart options

## `figure` arguments in Matplotlib

In Matplotlib, a key role in managing chart properties is played by the **figure** object, which represents the "canvas" on which all graphical elements are drawn. The `plt.figure()` and `plt.subplots()` functions allow you to customize various aspects of the figure. 

```python
plt.figure(num=None, figsize=None, dpi=None, facecolor=None, edgecolor=None, frameon=None, tight_layout=None)
```

Arguments:

| Argument       | Description                                                                                          | Default value     |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------|
| **`num`**       | Identification number or name of the figure (can be a number or a string).                  | `None` (automatic) |
| **`figsize`**   | Size of the figure in inches, given as a tuple `(width, height)`.                          | `(6.4, 4.8)`          |
| **`dpi`**       | Resolution of the figure in dots per inch.                                       | `100`                 |
| **`facecolor`** | Background color of the figure (the whole canvas).                                                              | `white`               |
| **`edgecolor`** | Color of the figure's border.                                                                          | `white`               |
| **`frameon`**   | Whether the figure should have a frame (`True` or `False`).                                           | `True`                |
| **`tight_layout`** | Automatic adjustment of elements on the figure to avoid overlapping (`True` or `False`). | `False`               |


```{python}
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Creating a figure with dimensions of 10x5 inches
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with custom dimensions")
plt.show(block=True)
```


```{python}
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(dpi=200)  # Higher resolution
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with higher resolution")
plt.show(block=True)
```

```{python}
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(facecolor='lightblue', edgecolor='gray')  # Background and border color
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with a custom background")
plt.show(block=True)
```


```{python}
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(tight_layout=True)  # Automatic adjustment
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart with automatic layout")
plt.show(block=True)
```


```{python}
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(num="My Figure")  # Figure name
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Figure with a name")
plt.show(block=True)
plt.show()
```

## Styles

Matplotlib offers built-in styles that allow for quick and easy customization of the appearance of charts. Using the `plt.style.use` function, you can load a ready-made style, so that the charts gain a consistent and aesthetic appearance without the need to manually set all the parameters.

```python
plt.style.use(style)
```

```{python}
#| echo: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Using the 'ggplot' style
plt.style.use('ggplot')
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6])
plt.title("Chart in the ggplot style")
plt.show(block=True)

```

List of styles:

```python
print(plt.style.available)
```

Reference: <https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/style_sheets/style_sheets_reference.html>
